mnemonics.title: Remember PATH Purpose
mnemonics.content: P-A-T-H = Program Allocation Tool for Hunting executables - the shell hunts for programs in directories listed in PATH.

mnemonics.title: HOME Row
mnemonics.content: HOME points to your home - where you start. Just like in a game, HOME is your starting base.

mnemonics.title: PS1 = Prompt String 1
mnemonics.content: PS1 = Primary System Interactive prompt - the main prompt you see.

acronyms.usage: Directories searched when executing commands without full path

acronyms.expansion: U ser S ystem E nvironment R ecord
Home directory for Operating & Menu Environment

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a hallway (PATH) with multiple doors (directories). When you type 'ls', the system checks each door in order to find the 'ls' program.
Draw two circles - Shell variables inside only the current shell circle. Environment variables span both circles, extending to child processes.
Think of 'export' as handing down a family heirloom (variable) to your children (child processes). Without export, it's not inherited.
A flow chart: Login shell → .bash_profile. New terminal (non-login) → .bashrc. Both lead to customized shell environment.

analogies.analogy: Like environment settings in a video game - graphics quality, difficulty level, controls - they affect how the game (process) runs.
Like a table of contents in a book - the system looks through each 'chapter' (directory) in order to find the 'page' (command) it needs.
Like sending a memo to all departments (child processes) - without exporting, the memo stays in your office (current shell) only.
Like erasing a sticky note from a shared bulletin board - removes the variable from the environment completely.
